title: Who is the Best Masters Winner?
text:
“A tradition unlike any other.” When April rolls around, the immortal words of Jim Nantz, first coined in 1986, ring ever more true.
The green jackets, the joy, the heart break, the Pimento Cheese sandwiches. These traditions all make the weekend at Augusta National Golf Club all the more memorable. Unlike all other Majors in golf, the Masters is the only one that is played at the same place every single year: Augusta, Georgia.
First held in 1934, and every Sunday since (except 1943-1945 for a certain world conflict), we’ve tuned in to see the green jacket placed on the years’ Masters winner. There have been 56 different winners, with some golfers even winning multiple green jackets.
As we turn our sights to Sunday, though, I was faced with an interesting question: who’s the best? I mean, yes, they all are: they’ve won the Masters, which is much better than I have done… so far.
But it is nonetheless an interesting question. If you put them all into a round together, who are the names that might come out on top and score another victory at Augusta? That’s what I aim to find out.
For this, we’ll be looking at all Masters winners from 1997 to 2025. Why start at 1997? Well, a certain golfer named after a certain large striped cat won his first jacket that year, but we’ll get to him later.

Green Jackets, Multiple
Before we begin, it’s important to note that, while there have been 56 winners of the Masters, 18 of these have been won multiple times by the same people, but only 4 have won more than once since 1997.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggbump)
library(gt)

masters <- read_csv("Master's Winners New - Sheet1 copy.csv")

winners <- masters |>
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Scottie Scheffler" |
    PLAYER == "Bubba Watson" |
    PLAYER == "Phil Mickelson" |
    PLAYER == "Tiger Woods"
  )

multiple <- winners |> 
  select(PLAYER, `TOTAL SCORE`, `TOTAL PAR`, YEAR)

multiple |> 
  gt() |> 
  tab_header(
    title = "Who Has Multiple Green Jackets in Their Locker?",
    subtitle = "Augusta can be unforgiving. However, there are 4 men who, since 1997, were lucky enough to leave Masters Sunday as a champion multiple times."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Brian Streeter  |  **Source:** The Masters")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none")








Who Has Multiple Green Jackets in Their Locker?


Augusta can be unforgiving. However, there are 4 men who, since 1997, were lucky enough to leave Masters Sunday as a champion multiple times.


PLAYER

TOTAL PAR

By: Brian Streeter | Source: The Masters










The Tournament
At Augusta National, during the Masters, golfers play through 4 rounds of golf, with half of the players being cut after Round 2. Though they’re still playing the same course, scores can change drastically. For example, this year, Augusta has had the lowest amount of rain on record. So, because of the dry grass, the greens are much faster than normal. But, what if it rains overnight? The greens become a lot slower.
So, what do our winners’ scores look like from round to round?


Code
masters <- read_csv("Master's Winners New - Sheet1 copy.csv")


masterslong <- masters |> 
  mutate(playeryear = paste(PLAYER, YEAR)) |> 
  select(playeryear, R1, R2, R3, R4) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=-playeryear, 
    names_to = "Round",
    values_to = "Strokes"
  )

roryround <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Rory McIlroy 2025" 
  ) 


scottie22round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Scottie Scheffler 2022" 
  )

tiger19round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Tiger Woods 2019" 
  )

phil06round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Phil Mickelson 2006" 
  )

tiger97round <- masterslong |> 
  filter(
   playeryear == "Tiger Woods 1997" 
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=masterslong, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear), color = "lightgrey")+
  geom_point(data=masterslong, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear), color = "lightgrey", size = 4) +
  geom_bump(data=roryround, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Rory McIlroy, 2025"))+
   geom_point(data=roryround, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Rory McIlroy, 2025"), size = 4)+
geom_bump(data=scottie22round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Scottie Scheffler, 2022"))+
   geom_point(data=scottie22round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Scottie Scheffler, 2022"), size = 4)+ 
  geom_bump(data=tiger19round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Tiger Woods, 2019"))+
   geom_point(data=tiger19round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Tiger Woods, 2019"), size = 4)+ 
  geom_bump(data=tiger97round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Tiger Woods, 1997"))+
   geom_point(data=tiger97round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Tiger Woods, 1997"), size = 4)+
  geom_bump(data=phil06round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color = "Phil Mickelson, 2006"))+
   geom_point(data=phil06round, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group = playeryear, color ="Phil Mickelson, 2006"), size = 4)+
  scale_color_manual(
    name = "Golfer",
    values = c(
    "#00563F", # Rory McIlroy, 2025
    "#F99ABA", # Scottie Scheffler, 2022
    "#CFA94A", # Tiger Woods, 1997
    "#C7EA46", # Tiger Woods, 2019
    "#48AB51" # Phil Mickelson, 2006
  ))+
   labs(title="Round by Round, Stroke by Stroke", subtitle="Played over 4 days, the Masters can be unpredictable, and it can break hearts. It also requires \nconsistency. So, how do our winners' scores look round by round?", caption="Source: The Masters | By Brian Streeter") +
  theme_minimal()











Round 4: “Hello, Friends”
This is it. The final day of the Masters. By the end of this day, one golfer will come out on top. They’ll put on the highly coveted green jacket, and hoist the trophy above their head in pure triumph.
Sunday is what makes the Masters so memorable. The towering Georgia Pines, the radiant Azaleas, the pristine fairways. Only the best of the best make it to Sunday, and only the best wins it all.
But, in order to do this - they have to get the lowest score - when all eyes are on them, alone.
So, when the pressure is at an all time high, who kept their score at an all time low?


Code
winners <- read_csv("Master's Winners New - Sheet1 copy.csv")

round4 <- winners |> 
  mutate(playeryear = paste(PLAYER, YEAR)) 

rory <-round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Rory McIlroy"
  )

tiger <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Tiger Woods"
  )

scottie <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Scottie Scheffler"
  )

jordan <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Jordan Spieth"
  )

phil <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Phil Mickelson"
  )

rahm <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Jon Rahm"
  )

dustin <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Dustin Johnson"
  )

bubba <- round4 |> 
  filter(
    PLAYER == "Bubba Watson"
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=round4, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="lightgrey")+
  geom_bar(data=rory, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill = "#9BCE6F")+
  geom_bar(data=tiger, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#7dc044")+
  geom_bar(data=scottie, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#00563F")+
  geom_bar(data=phil, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#17240c")+
  geom_bar(data=rahm, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#CEE7B8")+
  geom_bar(data=jordan, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#CFA94A")+
  geom_bar(data=dustin, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="lightgreen")+
   geom_bar(data=bubba, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, YEAR), weight=R4), fill="#F99ABA")+
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()+ 
  labs(
    x="Masters Winner", 
    y="Strokes", 
    title="Round 4: I Want a Sunday Kind of Love", 
    subtitle="Here we are. The Final Day of the Masters. Golfers that make it to Round 4 need to keep their scores low. \nSo, who was able to pack a new green jacket in their suitcase home?", 
    caption="Source: The Masters | By Brian Streeter"
    )+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 











The Verdict?
So, who is the best Masters winner?
Tiger Woods.
Is there really any question? Tiger has won 5 individual Masters championships, second only to Jack Nicklaus with 6. (‘63, ‘65, ‘66, ‘72, ‘75, ‘86)
Tiger has been a staple of the game, and made it what it is today.

title: How Did Rory McIlroy Win His Second Masters?
text:
The Masters Tournament has, without a doubt, become the most legendary golf tournament in history. Every year, we return to Augusta National Golf Club to watch the best golfers in the world competing for a green jacket on one of the most difficult courses around.
You watch The Masters for the tradition: the pimento cheese sandwiches, Amen corner, and an Egg Salad hat, but the reason you watch it again? To see which golfer will be able to score themselves a green jacket on Sunday.
Since 1934, 56 golfers have been able to claim the Augusta trophy. However, only 4 men in history have been able to win that trophy back to back, with the most recent inductee being Rory McIlroy, as he scored his second jacket in 2026.
To put this into perspective, 12 people have walked on the moon. Yes, you read that right, more people have walked on the moon than won the Masters back-to-back.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

multiple <- read_csv("project2data copy.csv")

double <- multiple |> 
  select(Player, `Total Score`, `Total Par`, Year)

double |> 
  mutate(
    `Total Par` = case_when(
      `Total Par` == "E" ~ "0",
      TRUE ~ `Total Par`
    )) |> 
  mutate(
    `Total Par` = as.numeric(`Total Par`)
  ) |> 
  
  
  gt()  |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Who are the, now, Four Men Who Have Won Two Green Jackets in Two Attempts?",
    subtitle = "Rory McIlroy joins an elite group of Masters winners who have defended their title, and won back-to-back tournaments."
  )  |> 
  
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Brian Streeter  |  **Source:** The Masters")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  
  
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |> 
   data_color(
    columns = c(`Total Par`),
    colors = scales::col_numeric(
      palette = c(
        "white", "#A4D1A2", "#006747"),
      domain = NULL)
  )








Who are the, now, Four Men Who Have Won Two Green Jackets in Two Attempts?


Rory McIlroy joins an elite group of Masters winners who have defended their title, and won back-to-back tournaments.


Player
Total Score
Total Par
Year




Rory McIlroy
276
-12
2026


Rory McIlroy
277
-11
2025


Tiger Woods
272
-16
2001


Tiger Woods
276
-12
2002


Nick Faldo
283
-5
1989


Nick Faldo
278
-10
1990


Jack Nicklaus
271
-17
1965


Jack Nicklaus
288
0
1966



By: Brian Streeter | Source: The Masters








Rory McIlroy, no doubt, joins an elite club. However, if you were to go to coma patient and tell them that Rory McIlroy, yes, that Rory McIlroy just won two back-to-back Masters tournaments, they’d probably turn to you and say, “Rory McIlroy? 2011 Masters meltdown Rory McIlroy?”
So, how did Rory pull off this legendary stat? It’s important to know Rory’s growing familiarity with Augusta National after his 2025 win. Rory mentioned during a press pool during this year’s tournament that he would often drop his daughter, Poppy, off at school, fly to Augusta and play a round, then be back home in time for supper.
Clearly, Rory had this back-to-back title in mind as soon as he won his first jacket, saying “I can’t wait to be back here next year (…) hopefully putting the green jacket back on myself.” But, did spending all this extra time at Augusta improve his play?


Code
library(ggrepel)

data <- read_csv("project2data copy.csv")

datalong <- data |> 
  mutate(playeryear = paste(Player, Year)) |> 
  select(playeryear, R1, R2, R3, R4) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=-playeryear, 
    names_to = "Round",
    values_to = "Strokes"
  )

rory25 <- datalong |> 
  filter(playeryear == "Rory McIlroy 2025")

rory26 <- datalong |> 
  filter(playeryear == "Rory McIlroy 2026")

rory25label <- rory25 |> filter(Round == "R4")

rory26label <- rory26 |> filter(Round == "R4")

ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=datalong, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=datalong, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear), color="grey")+
  geom_line(data=rory25, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear), color="#F660AB") +
  geom_point(data=rory25, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear), color="#F660AB")+
  geom_line(data=rory26, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear), color="#006747") +
  geom_point(data=rory26, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear), color="#006747")+
  geom_text_repel(data=rory25label, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear, label=playeryear)) + 
  geom_text_repel(data=rory26label, aes(x=Round, y=Strokes, group=playeryear, label=playeryear)) + 
 
   labs(
    title="Next Up on Tee 1: Rory McIlroy ... again", 
    subtitle="When one wins the Masters, they are able to play Augusta whenever they want. Rory mentioned he has played\nthe course quite often since his win last year. Clearly, he had been practicing, but did that practice really pay off?", 
    caption="Source: The Masters | By Brian Streeter"
    ) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
    theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Rory tends to have his best play on Friday, during Round 2, even saying this year that he “did most of (his) work on Thursday and Friday,” referring to aforementioned rounds. However, in both years there is a sharp up-tick at Round 4.
This has almost cost Rory his jacket twice; in 2025 going into a playoff with Justin Rose, and in 2026, finishing only one stroke above Scottie Sheffler, despite leading the field by 6 strokes going into the weekend.
So, this begs the question, what portion of McIlroy’s final score is made up by his play on Friday and Sunday?


Code
data <- read_csv("project2data copy.csv")

datarory <- data |>
  mutate(playeryear = paste(Player, Year)) 

rorydata <- datarory |>  
  group_by(playeryear, R2) |> 
  filter(
    playeryear == "Rory McIlroy 2025" |
    playeryear == "Rory McIlroy 2026") 

rorylongdata <- rorydata |> 
  select(playeryear, Year, `Total Score`, R2, R4) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols=c(-playeryear, -`Total Score`, -Year), 
    names_to = "Round",
    values_to = "Strokes" 
  ) |> 
  mutate(RoundYear = paste(Round, Year))

rorylongdata$RoundYear <- factor(rorylongdata$RoundYear, levels=c("R2 2025", "R4 2025", "R2 2026", "R4 2026"))


ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data = rorylongdata, aes(x=reorder(playeryear, -Year), weight=Strokes, fill=fct_rev(RoundYear))) + 
  coord_flip()+
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Round:", 
    values = c("#006747", "#A4D1A2", "#006747", "#A4D1A2"), 
    labels = c("Round 2 2025", "Round 4 2025", "Round 2 2026", "Round 4 2026")
    )+ 
  
  labs(
    x="", 
    y="Score", 
    title="McIlroy: Good at Golf Every Other Day", 
    subtitle="Rory's most noteable moments come in Round Two, where he builds his lead, and in Round 4, where he almost \nloses the lead he worked so hard to create. But how much do these rounds affect his total score?", 
    caption="Source: The Masters | By Brian Streeter"
    ) +
  
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Though his final score shows only a 1 stroke improvement from 2025 and 2026, Rory showed a 3 stroke improvement in his play during Rounds Two and Four.

Conclusion
With his Masters win in 2025, Rory McIlroy cemented himself into golf legend, being only the 6th golfer in history to complete the career grand slam. Now, with his 2026 win, being only the 4th golfer in Augusta records to defend his title, he has now made the golf world ask: ‘is Rory McIlroy one of the greatest golfers in history?’
It’s certainly worth the question. Maybe he is. There can be no doubt, however, that he certainly has changed the way the game is played.
All I can say is: Rory McIlroy needs to start planning his second champions dinner.
